arXiv:2306.07516v1 [cs.IT] 13 Jun 2023
WEIGHT HIERARCHIES OF 3-WEIGHT LINEAR
CODES FROM TWO p-ARY QUADRATIC FUNCTIONS
XIUMEI LI AND FEI LI
Abstract. The weight hierarchy of a linear code has been an
important research topic in coding theory since Wei’s original work
in 1991. Choosing D =
n
(x, y) ∈

Fps1 × Fps2
/
{(0, 0)} : f(x) +
g(y) = 0
o
as a deﬁning set , where f(x), g(y) are quadratic forms
over Fpsi, i = 1, 2, respectively, with values in Fp, we construct a
family of 3-weight p-ary linear codes and determine their weight
distributions and weight hierarchies completely. Most of the codes
can be used in secret sharing schemes.
1. Introduction
Let p be an odd prime number and Fps be the ﬁnite ﬁeld with ps
elements. Denote by F∗
ps the set of the nonzero elements of Fps.
Let C be a k-dimensional subspace of Fn
p. It is called an [n, k, d] pary linear code of length n with minimum (Hamming) distance d. For
1 ≤r ≤k, the concept of generalized Hamming weight (GHW) dr(C)
of C can be viewed as an extension of Hamming weight. We recall the
deﬁnition of GHWs of linear codes. Let [C, r]p be the set of all the
Fp-vector subspaces with dimension r. For H ∈[C, r]p, the support
Supp(H) of H is the set of coordinates where not all codewords of H
are zero, that is,
Supp(H) =
n
i : 1 ≤i ≤n, ci ̸= 0 for some c = (c1, c2, · · · , cn) ∈H
o
.
The r-th generalized Hamming weight of C, which is also called the
r-th minimum support weight, is
dr(C) = min
n
|Supp(H)| : H ∈[C, r]p
o
, 1 ≤r ≤k.
It is easy to see that d = d1(C). The sequence {d1(C), d2(C), · · · , dk(C)}
is called the weight hierarchy of C.
2020 Mathematics Subject Classiﬁcation. 94B05 and 11T71.
Key words and phrases. Linear code; Quadratic form; Weight distribution;
Weight hierarchy; Generalized Hamming weight.
1

2
XIUMEI LI AND FEI LI
The generalized Hamming weight was introduced in 1977 by Helleseth, Kløve et al. [8, 14]. Victor Wei [26] proved that they can characterize the cryptography performance of a linear code over the wiretap
channel of type II. From then on, much more attention was paid to generalized Hamming weights. The weight hierarchies for some well-known
classes of codes were determined, such as Hamming codes, Reed-Muller
codes, Reed-Solomon codes and Golay codes. To determine the weight
hierarchy of linear codes is relatively challenging. During the past three
decades, there were research results about weight hierarchies of some
classes of linear codes [7, 11, 26, 27, 12, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 22, 29, 30, 9].
A generic construction of linear codes was proposed by Ding et al.
[3, 4, 5, 6] as below. Denote by Trs the trace function from Fps onto
Fp. Let D = {d1, d2, · · · , dn} be a subset of F∗
ps. A p-ary linear code of
length n is deﬁned as follows:
CD = {(Trs(xd1), Trs(xd2), . . . , Trs(xdn)) : x ∈Fps},
(1.1)
and D is called the deﬁning set of CD. Li et al. [15] extended Ding’s
deﬁning-set construction, they constructed a p-ary linear codes CD by
CD = {(Trs(ax + by))(x,y)∈D : a, b ∈Fps},
(1.2)
where D is a subset of F2
ps\{(0, 0)}. Furthermore, many linear codes
have been obtained from some cryptographic functions in above two
construction method, some optimal linear codes with a few weights
can be constructed (see [2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 24, 15, 13, 19, 20, 9, 23] and the
references therein).
In this paper, we ﬁx the following notation. Let si be positive integers, qi = psi, i = 1, 2. Denote F = Fq1 × Fq2, F⋆= F
/
{(0, 0)} and
s = s1 + s2. Deﬁne a p-ary linear code CD as follows:
CD =
n
Trs1(xxi) + Trs2(yyi)

(xi,yi)∈D : (x, y) ∈F
o

(1.3)
where
D =
n
(x, y) ∈F⋆: f(x) + g(y) = 0
o
,
(1.4)
where f(x), g(y) are quadratic forms over Fqi, i = 1, 2, respectively,
with values in Fp. We will study the weight distribution and weight
hierarchy of CD in (1.3).
For the linear codes CD deﬁned in (1.3), a general formula is likely
be employed to calculate the generalized Hamming weight dr(CD). It
is presented in the following lemma.

WEIGHT HIERARCHIES

In Section 2, we set the main notation and give some properties
of p-ary quadratic forms. In Section 3, we introduce the parameters
of 3-weight linear codes derived from two p-ary quadratic forms over
diﬀerent ﬁnite ﬁelds and determine their weight hierarchies completely.
Section 4 completes the paper.

results on cyclotomic ﬁeld Q(ζp).

4. Concluding Remarks
Inspired by the works of [15, 20], using the extended deﬁning-set
method, we constructed a family of 3-weight linear codes in (1.3). Our
deﬁning-set is D =
n
(x, y) ∈

Fps1 ×Fps2
/
{(0, 0)} : f(x)+g(y) = 0
o
,
f(x), g(y) are p-ary quadratic forms over Fpsi, i = 1, 2. By exponential
sum theory together with our previous formula of generalized Hamming
weight, we determined their weight distributions and weight hierarchies
completely.
Let wmin and wmax denote the minimum and maximum nonzero
weight of our obtained code CD deﬁned in (1.3), respectively. If R ≥3,
then it can be easily checked that
wmin
wmax
> p −1
p
.
By the results in [1] and [31], we know that every nonzero codeword of
CD is minimal and most of the codes we constructed are suitable for
constructing secret sharing schemes with interesting properties.
